TextENGNew: There is something about that child I cannot warm to.
Only by writing these words down do I first confront the truth.
No parenting book needs to tell me that a child is not a parent's doll.
changeReason: Original mistranslates JPN as 'They never warn you in books' when it actually means 'without needing a book to tell me'; also 'plaything' is vaguer than JPN 人形 (doll). New translation is accurate.

TextENGNew: A parent who loses all affection the moment a child ceases to bend to their will has no right to be a parent.
changeReason: Old reverses the subject: says children can't 'feel loved' but Japanese says the PARENT stops feeling affection.

TextENGNew: This is not that.
How can I put it...@k...In fact, it may be harder to express in writing.
I only ask that my child be average.
As long as she is not falling behind, she need not excel.@kAge-appropriate sensibilities are all I require.
But that child has been different since kindergarten.
While her classmates ran about, unable to contain their excitement over the next day's field trip, she wore a bored expression, standing apart from the group.
When the props for the sports festival were accidentally broken and the other children were desperately apologizing, she alone wore a bored expression, standing apart from the group.
When the teacher was reading a fun picture book, only she wasn't smiling.
Even if they all got to eat a delicious bento, only she wasn't happy.
...If it were only this, I could still manage to understand.
What I cannot fathom is that sometimes, when faced with exactly the same situation, she would suddenly respond with age-appropriate delight.
I don't understand the principle behind that, as a parent.
Why was she indifferent to that field trip but excited about this one?
Why were they indifferent about that picture book, but happy about this one?
Why were they indifferent about that bento, but happy about this one?
The former and the latter looked identical to me.
...Sometimes the former even seemed superior.
I cannot understand that child's sensibilities.
At parent-teacher conferences, the teacher confessed the very same bewilderment.
And I would always respond that I don't understand my own child, and we would mope together.
My husband, the optimist, said young children's sensibilities differ from adults', so there was no need to worry if we couldn't understand everything.@k...I lamented his lack of urgency.
One day, when I was in a good mood.
I took the effort to prepare a menu that my child would enjoy.
...Yet she only smiled with a vague, bored expression.
My temper flared at the sight, and I struck her on the head.
On a day when the weather was nice.
The laundry I had just hung up to dry was blown by a large gust of wind, causing the entire line to fall down.
...But she, watching me trying to pick up the laundry in a panic, stood there laughing.
That attitude frankly made me angry, so I hit her on the head.
I think the same sort of thing happened countless times.
Before I knew it, that child would only show me that bored expression.
...I reflected on what a poor mother I had been.
Thinking I should regain her trust, I thought to get it back little by little with some light interactions.
Seeing her do some sort of craftwork on the veranda, I called out to her.
``These past few days have been nice and sunny. The air feels nice, doesn't it?``
The child looked up at me with that expression -- the bored, indifferent one I hated most -- then wordlessly returned her gaze to her hands and lost herself once more in her craft.
...In the past, that gesture alone would have earned a smack.@k...I held myself back.
``What are you making? Is it a doll?``
``......A weather charm.``
That child was using inserts from the newspaper to deftly make some weather charms.
The forecast hadn't called for rain.
But that child, in her own way, was praying that these sunny days would continue.
Being able to understand my own child for the first time in a while, I couldn't hide my own happiness.
I fetched a ball of yarn and hung my child's charming little teru-teru-bouzu from the eaves.

``Ahahaha... It's no good, Rika.@k@vS20/00/huruM44004. The heads are too heavy, see?@k@vS20/00/huruM44005. They've become reverse weather charms now.@k@vS20/00/huruM44006. If they're like this, they'll call the rain instead of the sun.``
When I started to take them down, the child tugged at the hem of my clothes as if to stop me.
``......That's because I made them to be upside-down. They're fine.``
``...But, Rika. If they're upside-down, they won't be a charm that calls for a sunny day, you know?``
"I'm doing a charm to make it rain, so that's fine."
...I desperately fought down the emotions surging inside me. I was trying so hard to understand my child.
``Oh... I see... The morning glories in the garden were starting to get a little wilted from all the sun, so you wanted some rain, right?``
The child turned to me with that expression -- the one I hated most of all.
``I got bored... of the clear weather.``
...I don't understand. I don't understand.@k...I simply cannot understand that child...
